text: An additional five-millimeter assistant port is placed to introduce a five-millimeter, third-degree scope, which tracks the movements of the robotic third arm used for retraction during the procedure.

The third robotic arm is positioned in the left lower quadrant for retraction during the robotic left hemicolectomy.

Following entry into the abdomen, the surgical team explores the abdominal cavity using robotic arms and a laparoscopic scope.

The surgeon begins by taking down adhesions to the anterior abdominal wall, using robotic instruments to carefully separate the fibrous tissue from the abdominal surface.

The robotic arm grasps the colon and retracts it anteriorly and laterally, providing counter traction that facilitates easier and safer dissection of the surrounding tissues.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to perform high ligation of the vascular pedicle, visible through the robotic camera view.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to take down the retroperitoneal attachments of the sigmoid colon, following the completion of previous dissection steps.

The surgeon uses the robotic arms to lift the mesentery of the colon upwards while ensuring the left ureter and gonadal vessels remain positioned below, maintaining clear separation between these structures.

The surgeon mobilizes the ascending colon laterally along the white line of Toldt, progressing upwards towards the splenic flexure, with visible separation of the colon from the lateral attachments.

The surgeon dissects and divides the mesorectum at the level of the promontory, carefully transecting the superior hemorrhoidal vessels.

The surgeon assesses the colon to confirm adequate length and blood supply, visually inspecting the vascular integrity and positioning of the colon.

The surgeon opens the colon and places an EEA anvil into the descending colon, ensuring there is adequate length for the procedure.

The surgeon closes the colotomy with sutures, carefully stitching the opened section of the colon.

The surgeon transects the colon proximally using a stapler and performs a stapled end-to-end anastomosis, connecting the proximal and distal segments of the colon.

The surgeon checks the integrity of the anastomosis by inspecting the circular tissue 'donuts' and performs an air leak test to ensure there are no leaks at the site of the stapled EEA anastomosis.
